# SEV-1 INCIDENT - RELEASE BLOCKER
**Title:** Statements are filing some payments under the wrong kind of transaction, or dropping the payee name
**Owner:** Payments Operations
**Disposition:** Do not ship this release until it is resolved

## What customers reported

Over the past few days support has collected a run of tickets that all share one
shape: the money itself moved fine, but the app files the payment under a category
that does not match what the customer actually did, or shows the wrong name - or
no name at all - beside it. In every case the row that misbehaves carries one very
particular, uncommon form of raw statement text; everyday rows are labeled exactly
as before. The tickets below are anonymized, but the raw narration each customer
pasted is reproduced verbatim, because that raw text is what makes each case
reproducible.

> "I wrote a cheque against my account and the app just lists it as a general
> transfer now - nothing marks it as a cheque anymore. My earlier cheques were
> tagged correctly. The only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own
> number, nothing else around it."

> "My salary came in and the amount is right, but where my employer's name should
> be the app now just prints the word `NACH`. The raw text on that row looks like
> `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` - it used to lift the employer's name off the end
> of that reference, and now it is picking up the wrong piece of it."

> "On some transfers that come in, the sender's name is blank now. Those rows all
> share the same reference layout - a short block of letters, then a long string
> of digits, then the name - for example `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`. The name
> sitting at the end just is not being pulled through anymore; the name column
> comes out empty."

The same batch of complaints also covers two quieter cases that are about the
category rather than the name. The token confirmation charge a bank posts when a
new auto-debit mandate is set up - a small nominal amount, with the note flagging
it as a mandate - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being
recognized as a verification entry. And a refund that came back on a card is
landing as a plain refund for some customers when it should read as a card
reversal; a customer on a different type of account sees the same kind of refund
labeled correctly, so the wrong label appears to depend on the account category.

## Scope of impact

Everyday activity - normal purchases, routine transfers, regular deposits - is
categorized and named correctly, which is precisely why this slipped through QA and
why our monitoring dashboards looked healthy. The breakage is confined to a small
set of unusual transaction forms - among them a note or reference that is exactly
a certain number of digits wide - each one hinging on some exact shape of the raw
row rather than anything about the customer, and each form individually uncommon. But they occur right across the customer base, so they
generate a steady trickle of "why is this labeled wrong" tickets and, more
damaging, they poison the spending-category summaries and payee histories that
several downstream features rely on. That combination is why we are treating it
as ship-blocking rather than a backlog item.

## What we need from engineering

The labeling path is fully deterministic and runs offline - hand it the same
transaction and it reproduces the same wrong answer every time - so each of these
is reproducible on demand and can be pinned down exactly. Get the affected forms
labeling and naming correctly again, and do it without disturbing any transaction
that is already labeled right today. A fix that rescues these cases but nudges the
behavior of ordinary, currently-correct transactions is not acceptable: the pass
condition is simply that the specific problem payments described above come out
right and everything else stays exactly where it is.
I'll investigate this SEV-1 incident. Let me start by understanding the codebase structure and finding the transaction labeling/categorization logic.

Search the codebase very thoroughly. Look for:

Report back the exact file paths and line numbers of the relevant functions/logic. Include code snippets of the key functions that do categorization and name extraction. Be very thorough - explore multiple directories and naming conventions.

# Search Report: Transaction Categorization & Payee/Name Extraction

## Overview of Architecture

This is a **Python/Flask** application (`categorizationapp`) that processes bank transactions. The entire codebase lives under:
```
/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/
```

The processing pipeline is orchestrated in `Controller.py` and runs in this order per transaction batch:
1. **`categorize_<BANK>_transactions(transactions, accountType)`** → assigns the "kind" (`transactionCategory`, `transactionSubcategory`, `merchantType`) — **this is the categorization logic you asked about (#1)**
2. **`purpose(transactions, accountType)`** → refines purpose
3. **`clean_names(transactions, accountType, bank)`** → assigns/normalizes `partyName` — **this is the payee/sender name extraction (#2)**
4. `internal_payment`, `salary_detection`, `pfm_categorization`, `simple_description` → optional post-processing

The pattern is consistent: every module builds a list of `[condition, choice]` pairs and applies them via `np.select(...)` with a default fallback.

---

## Directory Structure

**Per-bank scripts** (`/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/`) — 39 files, one per bank. Each contains BOTH categorization AND name extraction for that bank:
`HDFC.py`, `ICICI.py`, `SBI.py`, `Axis.py`, `Kotak.py`, `PNB.py`, `BOI.py`, `IDBI.py`, `IDFC.py`, `UnionBank.py`, `BOB.py`, `Canara.py`, `Federal.py`, `Citi.py`, `StandardCharted.py`, `Indusind.py`, `KarurVyasa.py`, `SouthIndian.py`, `UCO.py`, `YES.py`, `CityUnion.py`, `BankOfMaharashtra.py`, `Karnataka.py`, `Syndicate.py`, `RBL.py`, `DCB.py`, `Paytm.py`, `Andhra.py`, `Central.py`, `Bandhan.py`, `AuSmallFinanceBank.py`, `Saraswat.py`, `SVC.py`, `TamilnadMerchantile.py`, `Indian.py`, `KarurVyasa.py`

**Shared utility scripts** (`/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/UtilityScripts/`):
- `RegexConstants.py` — merchant/category keyword regexes (NACH, refund, reversal category tokens live in bank scripts; merchant keyword lists here)
- `BankRegexConstants.py` — **the reference-format regex patterns (digit counts, letter blocks) you asked about**
- `CleanName.py` — global payee-name normalization (1971 lines)
- `AppUtil.py` — the core string-slicing helpers (`sep_by_`, `py_extract`, etc.)
- `PFMCategorization.py` — the personal-finance-management category layer
- `Purpose.py`, `CardPurpose.py`, `CardCleanNames.py`, `InternalPayment.py`, `SalaryDetection.py`, `SimpleDescription.py`, `RecurrentTxns.py`
- `TransactionCategoryEnums.py`, `TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py` — the enumerated "kind" values

---

## #1 — CATEGORIZATION (assigning "kind"/category)

### Core mechanism (all bank scripts)
Each bank's `categorize_*` function builds `conditions_choices` and does:
```python
transactions['transactionCategory'] = np.select(df['conditions'], df['choices'], default="TRANSFER")
transactions['transactionSubcategory'] = np.select(...)   # second pass
transactions['merchantType'] = np.select(...)             # third pass
```

### Representative example — `BankScripts/HDFC.py`
- **`categorize_HDFC_transactions`** — defined at **line 9**
- Category assignment (`transactionCategory`): **lines 11–104** (the `conditions_choices` list, applied at line 104)
- Subcategory assignment (`transactionSubcategory`): **lines 112–132**
- `merchantType` assignment: **lines 134–139**

Key token-handling lines (NACH/ACH/cheque/mandate/refund/reversal):
```python
# HDFC.py line 19 — REFUND tokens
[transactions.description.str.contains("UPI.*REFUND|IMPS.*REFUND|^REF-|^RREF|PAYAIR|-REFUND-|WAIVER|IOC REF NO|EXGRATIA REFUND", na=False, case=False) & transactions.type.eq("CREDIT") , "REFUND"],
# HDFC.py line 25 — REVERSAL tokens
[transactions.description.str.contains("[Rr][Ee][Vv][Ee][Rr][Ss][Aa][Ll]|\\bREV\\b|\\bRVS[DL]|REVERSED|REV_SI|CHGS? REVERS|\\bDD CANCELLED|^REV_FEE", na=False, case=False) , "REVERSAL"],
# HDFC.py line 51 — AUTO_PAYMENT (ACH/SI/mandate) tokens
[transactions.description.str.contains("^ACH D-|^SI\\b|AUTOPAY \\bSI\\b|^EMI.*C[Hh][Qq]|...|ACH D- TP ACH", ...) & ... , "AUTO_PAYMENT"],
# HDFC.py line 71 — cheque
[transactions.description.str.contains("\\b[Cc][Hh][Qq]\\b|^I/W CHQ RETURN|[Cc]heque|CHEQUE", ...) & transactions.type.eq("DEBIT") , "CHEQUE_PAID"],
```

### NACH / ACH / mandate / reversal handling across banks
The tokens you specifically named appear in nearly every bank script. Notable locations:
- **`BankScripts/UnionBank.py`** lines 18, 24, 40–41 (`^NACH`, `NACHRTN`, `^REV/NACH/`)
- **`BankScripts/SBI.py`** lines 23, 29, 33, 48–49 (`ACHDr`, `CMP MANDATE`, `E mandate`, `NACH00`)
- **`BankScripts/PNB.py`** lines 14, 22, 37, 40 (`NACH E MANDATE`, `^NACH-\s?DR`, `^ACH/`)
- **`BankScripts/Kotak.py`** lines 12, 44–45, 89 (`^NACH-`, `ECSICR`, `^NACH-SAL`)
- **`BankScripts/ICICI.py`** lines 18, 32 (`REV_NACH`, `NACH_CHRG`)
- **`BankScripts/StandardCharted.py`** lines 24, 59–61 (`REV ACH DR`, `NACH DR IW`)
- Bounce/penalty tokens ("ECS/NACH Bounce", "ECS_PENALTY") defined in `UtilityScripts/TransactionCategoryEnums.py` line 34, 37, 167 and `TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py` line 59.

### Bank-charge / penalty regex constants (used by categorization)
`UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py`:
- `ECS_PENAL` — **line 111** (`NACH:RTN:CHRG|ECS:RTN:|ECSRTN...`)
- `ECS_PENAL_YES` — **line 387** (`ACH RTN CHG|NACH TXN RTN CHRG|MANDATE RTN CHRGS...`)
- Per-bank `BANK_CHARGES_*`, `REVERSAL_*`, `CHQ_RTN_*` constants throughout (lines 70, 105, 168, 323, 344, 390–394, etc.)

### The enumerated "kind" values
- **`UtilityScripts/TransactionCategoryEnums.py`** — full enum of transaction categories (e.g. `ECS="ECS/NACH"` line 34, `NACH="NACH"` line 37)
- **`UtilityScripts/TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py`** — subcategory enum

### PFM category layer
- **`UtilityScripts/PFMCategorization.py`**, function **`pfm_categorization`** at **line 8**. Maps `transactionCategory`/`transactionSubcategory`/`merchantType` into `pfmCategoryOne` (lines 14–81), `pfmCategoryTwo` (lines 83–191), and `pfmCategoryType` (INCOME/EXPENSE/TRANSFER, lines 197–204).

---

## #2 — PAYEE / SENDER NAME EXTRACTION

### Two-stage design:
**Stage A — raw extraction from narration (per-bank scripts):** Each bank's `categorize_*` function has a SECOND `conditions_choices` block that slices the raw `description` to pull out the name, writing to `partyName`.

**Stage B — normalization (global):** `CleanName.py::clean_names` maps the extracted raw names to canonical merchant/brand names.

### Stage A — raw name extraction — Representative: `BankScripts/HDFC.py`
- Name extraction block: **lines 146–221** (applied to `partyName` at line 221)
- Post-processing/cleanup passes: **lines 223–273**
- Uses helpers from `AppUtil.py` (`sep_by_dash`, `sep_by_`, `py_extract`, etc.) and pattern constants from `BankRegexConstants.py`.

Key snippets showing digit-count / letter-block reference parsing:
```python
# HDFC.py line 157 — NEFT ref format: 11-char alnum block + 7+ digit block
[transactions.description.str.contains("^NEFT [CD][Rr]-[A-z0-9]{11}-[0-9]{7,}-", ...) , sep_by_dash(transactions.description,3,4)],
# HDFC.py line 122 (StandardCharted equivalent) / HDFC line 214-215 — remark digit-count checks
[transactions.remark.str.contains("^N[0-9]{15}$", ...) , transactions.description],
[transactions.remark.str.contains("^[0-9]{16}$", ...) , transactions.description],
# HDFC.py line 169 — NACH auto-pay name extraction
[transactions.description.str.contains("^ACH D- BD-NACH-", ...), sep_by_dash(transactions.description,3,4).replace(to_replace='\\d', value="", regex=True).str.strip()],
```

### The core string-slicing helpers — `UtilityScripts/AppUtil.py`
- `sep_by_(description, n1, n2)` — split on `/`, join slice — **line 14**
- `sep_by_dash` — split on `-` — **line 17**
- `sep_by_wave` (`~`), `sep_by_star`, `sep_by_comma`, `sep_by_colon`, `sep_by_under`, bracket variants — **lines 20–45**
- `py_extract(description, pat, index)` — regex `.str.extract()` with null-handling — **line 61**
- `fuzzy_match` (fuzzywuzzy) — **line 48**

### The reference-format regex patterns (digit counts / letter blocks) — `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py`
This is the file most relevant to your "regex patterns that match transaction reference formats" request:
- **POS patterns** — `HDFC_POS_PATTERN` line 76 (`^POS [0-9]+[X]+[0-9]+[X]*[0-9]* (.*)`), `SBI_CP_PATTERN*` lines 8–31 (SBI POS/PG with `[0-9]{4}`, `[A-Z]{2}[0-9]+` blocks)
- **Cheque/MICR patterns** — `HDFC_CHQ_PATTERN1–10` lines 81–90; `CHQ_PATTERN1–4` lines 314–318; `CHQ_CLEAN_KOTAK` line 318 (long bank-name list)
- **Fund-transfer patterns** — `HDFC_FT_PATTERN1–7` lines 93–99 (e.g. `^[0-9]{14}-([A-Z].*)` line 97, ` CR - [0-9]{14} - [A-Z]` line 98), `FT_PATTERN0–19` lines 241–260, `FT_CHQ_PATTERN1–37` lines 263–299
- **NEFT/RTGS/IMPS patterns** — `NEFT_PATTERN0–7` lines 206–213 (e.g. `(NEFT [A-Z]{3,5}|NEFT [A-z]{1}|NEFT \d)[0-9]+(.*)` line 209), `KT_RTGS_PATTERN` line 217, `IMPS_PATTERN1–8` lines 195–202, `UB_NEFT_*/UB_RTGS_*` (Union) lines 120–129
- **Auto-pay / ECS patterns** — `SBI_AUTOPAY1–9` lines 33–41 (e.g. `^DEBIT-?\s?ACHDr [A-Z]{4}[0-9]+ (.*)` line 33), `ECS_END` line 178, `ECS_START` line 179, `PNB_AUTO_1` line 159
- **Bank-name-stripping blocks** (letter blocks) — `NEFT_CLEAN_KOTAK` line 214, `NEFT_DEBIT_STOP` line 334, `REMOVE_BANK_CB` line 417, `REMOVE_BANK_CITI` line 424, `CHQ_CLEAN_SCB` line 432

### `BankScripts/ICICI.py` — another strong example of digit-count/letter-block parsing
- `^[A-Z]{5}XX[0-9]+ ` → AUTO_PAYMENT / LOAN_REPAYMENT — lines 86, 121
- `str.count("/")` slicing for INFT/NEFT/IMPS — lines 147, 154, 164–165
- `[A-Za-z]{4}[0-9]{7}` party2 checks for NEFT/IMPS name extraction — lines 228–229
- Junk-name suppression (`[0-9]{6,}|^[A-Z]{1,2}$`) — line 233

### Stage B — global name normalization — `UtilityScripts/CleanName.py`
- **`clean_names(transactions, accountType, bank)`** — defined at **line 9**
- Massive `conditions_choices` mapping raw `partyName`/`party2`/`description` substrings to canonical merchant names, grouped by domain: DTH/TV (lines 13–26), Internet (29–50), Electricity/utilities (53–120), ... continuing through wallets/banks/paytm/google (~lines 1850–1940). Final `np.select` at **line 1944**.
- Sets `merchantType` (lines 1948–1956), `transactionSubcategory` overrides (lines 1958–1967), and derives `party2` (spaceless key) at line 1946.
- Note bank-specific branch: **line 1939** uses the `bank` argument (`bank=="IDBI"`) for IDBI-specific name extraction.

---

## Account-type / category affecting labeling

The `accountType` (values: `SAVING`, `CURRENT`, `CREDIT_CARD`, `OVER_DRAFT`) affects both categorization and naming:

- **`Controller.py`** (`categorizeTransaction`, line 70): reads `accountType = requestPayload['acc_type']` (line 75). Routes `CREDIT_CARD` accounts through `card_clean_names` + `card_purpose` instead of the normal path (e.g. lines 94–98), and only runs `salary_detection` for `SAVING` (e.g. lines 106–108).
- **`BankScripts/HDFC.py`** uses `accountType` directly inside categorization:
  - line 124 — `TRANSFER`+6-digit credit ⇒ `SALARY` only when `accountType=="SAVING"`
  - line 125 — `SALARY_DISBURSED` only when `accountType=="CURRENT"`
  - line 126 — `INSUFFICIENTS_FUNDS_BOUNCE` gated on `accountType != "OVER_DRAFT"`
- **`CleanName.py`** — `clean_names` takes `accountType` and `bank` as parameters (line 9), so labeling is account-type/bank aware.
- **Credit-card-specific modules**: `UtilityScripts/CardCleanNames.py` (name extraction for CC statements) and `UtilityScripts/CardPurpose.py` (CC categorization).

---

## Summary of the most important files/functions to investigate for your bug

| Concern | File | Function / Lines |
|---|---|---|
| Categorization ("kind") — per bank | `BankScripts/<BANK>.py` | `categorize_<BANK>_transactions` (e.g. HDFC.py line 9, category block 11–104) |
| NACH/ACH/mandate/refund/reversal token matching | `BankScripts/*.py` + `BankRegexConstants.py` | e.g. HDFC.py 19/25/51/71; BankRegexConstants ECS_PENAL line 111 |
| Category enum values | `UtilityScripts/TransactionCategoryEnums.py` / `TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py` | lines 34, 37, 167 / line 59 |
| Raw payee/name extraction — per bank | `BankScripts/<BANK>.py` | 2nd `conditions_choices` block (HDFC.py 146–221; ICICI.py 137–234) |
| Reference-format regexes (digit counts, letter blocks) | `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` | POS 8–31/76, CHQ 81–90/314–318, FT 93–103/241–299, NEFT/RTGS/IMPS 195–218, AUTOPAY 33–41 |
| String slicing / extraction helpers | `UtilityScripts/AppUtil.py` | `sep_by_*` 14–45, `py_extract` 61 |
| Global name normalization | `UtilityScripts/CleanName.py` | `clean_names` line 9 (map applied at 1944) |
| Merchant keyword lists | `UtilityScripts/RegexConstants.py` | category keyword constants lines 5–190+ |
| PFM category layer | `UtilityScripts/PFMCategorization.py` | `pfm_categorization` line 8 |
| Account-type routing | `Controller.py` | `categorizeTransaction` line 70; account-type branches throughout |

If you can share the specific symptom (e.g., which bank, whether the wrong "kind" or the wrong payee name), I can narrow down to the exact offending condition line — the most likely culprits for miscategorization are the *ordering* of conditions in the per-bank `conditions_choices` lists (since `np.select` picks the first match), and for name extraction, the digit-count/letter-block regexes in `BankRegexConstants.py` combined with the `sep_by_*` index slicing.
</task_result>
</task>

steps.message:
**IDBI.py:110** stands out — it exactly matches the ticket format `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`:

```python
[transactions.description.str.contains("^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{10,}", ...) , py_extract(transactions.description, pat="[0-9]{10,} (.*)", index=1)],
```

`GBHDL` = 5 letters, `0123456789` = 10 digits, then ` RAVI KUMAR`. The pattern `^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{10,}` matches. The extraction `py_extract(..., pat="[0-9]{10,} (.*)", index=1)` — but note there's only ONE capture group `(.*)`. `py_extract` uses `.get(index)`. With a single group, `.str.extract` returns column index **0**, not 1. So `index=1` returns None → empty name!

Let me verify this is the bug.

Confirmed. Line 110: pattern `[0-9]{10,} (.*)` has exactly one capture group, so `.str.extract()` produces a DataFrame with a single column indexed `0`. Calling `.get(1)` returns `None` → `py_extract` returns empty string. The name is dropped. The fix: `index=0`.

Let me verify how py_extract behaves and confirm the bug, then check that index=0 gives the name.

Now I can see the structure clearly. Line 84 `^ACH-BD-` uses index 2,3 which for `ACH-BD-<NAME>` (3 parts) gives the name correctly. But line 83 `^ACH-BD-NACH` has an EXTRA `-NACH-` token, so the name is one dash further along — it needs index 3,4, not 2,3. The line 83 was clearly added to special-case the `NACH` variant but kept the wrong index (2,3 = "NACH").

The two banks that treat a mandate as an ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION (verification entry) based on nominal amount are:
- **Axis.py:25** — `transactions.amount.eq(1) & description contains MANDATE → ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`
- **Indusind.py:30** — `transactions.amount.eq(2) & description contains MANDATE → ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`

The symptom: "a small **nominal amount**, with the note flagging it as a mandate" — being filed as ordinary transfer instead of a verification entry. The tell is `amount.eq(1)` / `amount.eq(2)` — an **exact** amount check. A token confirmation charge is "a small nominal amount" — not necessarily exactly 1 or 2. If a bank posts, say, ₹1 in one and the other uses `.eq(2)`, or the nominal amount is a different small value, `.eq()` misses it → falls through to TRANSFER.

These two are inconsistent (one uses `eq(1)`, the other `eq(2)`). Let me look more carefully at both to identify which is the regression. The proper check for a "nominal amount" should be `amount.lt(...)` (like the mandate-charge lines that use `amount.lt(150)`). Let me look at both files' context.
